sn20.9:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
sn20.9:1.3: And on that occasion a certain newly-ordained monk was spending too much time visiting lay families.
sn20.9:1.5: “Friend, don’t spend so much time visiting lay families.”
sn20.9:1.6: He, admonished by the monks, said,
sn20.9:1.7: “But these elder monks suppose that lay families should be visited. So why not me?”
sn20.9:2.1: Then many monks went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As they were sitting there, they said to him,
sn20.9:2.2: “Lord, there is a certain newly-ordained monk who is spending too much time visiting lay families.
sn20.9:2.3: The monks have said to him,
sn20.9:2.4: ‘Friend, don’t spend so much time visiting lay families.’
sn20.9:2.5: He, admonished by the monks, said,
sn20.9:2.6: ‘But these elder monks suppose that lay families should be visited. So why not me?’”
sn20.9:3.1: [The Blessed One said:] “Once, monks, there was a great lake in the wilderness,
sn20.9:3.2: and bull elephants were living in dependence on it.
sn20.9:3.3: Plunging into the lake, they would pull up lotus roots & stalks with their trunks and—having washed them so that they were well washed, having chewed them when they were free of mud—would swallow them.
sn20.9:3.4: This led to beauty & strength for them, and from that cause there came neither death nor death-like pain.
sn20.9:3.5: “But young calf elephants, imitating them, plunged into the lake and pulled up lotus roots & stalks with their trunks. Without washing them so that they were well washed, they chewed them while they were still muddy and swallowed them.
sn20.9:3.6: This did not lead to beauty & strength for them,
sn20.9:3.7: and from that cause there came either death or death-like pain.
sn20.9:4.1: “In the same way, monks, there is the case where elder monks, early in the morning, having adjusted their lower robes and, carrying their bowls & outer robes, enter a village or town for alms.
sn20.9:4.3: The lay people, confident in them, do acts of confidence [give gifts].
sn20.9:4.4: They [the elder monks] partake of those gains unattached to them, uninfatuated, guiltless, seeing the drawbacks [of attachment to them], and discerning the escape from them.
sn20.9:4.5: This leads to beauty & strength for them, and from that cause there comes neither death nor death-like pain.
sn20.9:4.6: “But newly-ordained monks, imitating them, early in the morning, having adjusted their lower robes and, carrying their bowls & outer robes, enter a village or town for alms.
sn20.9:4.8: The lay people, confident in them, do acts of confidence.
sn20.9:4.9: They [the newly-ordained monks] partake of those gains attached to them, infatuated, guilty, not seeing the drawbacks [of attachment to them], and not discerning the escape from them.
sn20.9:4.10: This does not lead to beauty & strength for them, and from that cause there comes either death or death-like pain.
sn20.9:4.12: ‘We will partake [of our gains] unattached, uninfatuated, guiltless, seeing the drawbacks [of attachment to them], and discerning the escape from them.’
sn20.9:4.13: That’s how you should train yourselves.”
